text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).
label: neutro

text_clean: Se puede llegar en teleferico o en carro demoran 20 min y 1:30 horas aprox respectivamen, hace bastante frío, se puede subir a caballo o a pie que es una caminata de 25 min aprox tiene lugarea donde descansar, en la parte donde se contrata el caballo se puede comer algo hay de diversos precios y gustos, al llegar a la fortaleza se puede contratar un guia que seria lo recomendable por lo general se forma un grupo grande para que el precio por persona sea mas accesible nos cobraron unos 60 soles el servicio de guia a todo el grupo, recomiendo ir a primer turno porque no hay muchas personas y se tiene más libertad de sacar fotos, cosa a resaltar se debe respetar el sendero y que te pueden sacar de la fortaleza por un incumplimiento de normas (le pasó a una pareja que venia detras nuestro). En general una vista increible llena de historia y cultura.

I personally would recommend getting a tour guide as there is alot to learn from these 2000 year old ruins. However, it's also nice having a wander round as these ruins are so spectacular.

text_clean: Watching the Nazca Lines from the skies is a one in a lifetime opportunity. There are so many lines that the tours have chosen to show only the most impressive ones. Despite numerous theories, so far no one has been able to understand why these lines were marked on the soil as they are only perceptible from the sky. The lines were amazing to see, the flight less enjoyable as it was tourning a lot. Three out of five passagers got sick... I also wished that the flight was conducted a bit closer to the ground to improve visibility of the lines. It's probably a safety measure though.

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: Situated on the outskirts of the historic city of Ollantaytambo, the Ollantaytambo Archaeological Site stands as one of the most captivating and beautiful destinations in the Sacred Valley. Offering panoramic views and captivating walks amidst ancient Inca architecture, the site boasts remnants of the legendary Templo del Sol (Temple of the Sun) and terraces dating back to the Inca era, originally built for agricultural purposes. During the Inca period, Ollantaytambo served as a crucial 'tambo,' a resting place for travelers along the Inca Trail. It marked the final stop before continuing the journey to Machu Picchu, as well as the first stop upon departing from the famed citadel. Additionally, it functioned as a vital storage facility for grains and crops. Steeped in history, Ollantaytambo also played a significant role during the Spanish conquest, serving as a stronghold for the Inca resistance. During our visit, we were mesmerized by the site's magic, spending approximately 45 to 60 minutes exploring its wonders. While souvenir and collectible shops line the entrance gate, unfortunately, we didn't have the opportunity to peruse them.
label: positivo

text_clean: Un lugar mágico. Accesible y barato. Si estás en Tarapoto, toma una moto al pequeño terminal de colectivos que se encuentra en el distrito Banda de Shilcayo (a 4 minutos de la Plaza de Armas de Tarspoto o a 5 minutos del aeropuerto). Luego, toma una de las camionetas que se van a barranquita y pagas 10 soles (cerca de tres dólares) para que te deje en las cataratas de Aguasiyacu. El viaje demora entre 20 y 30 minutos. La entrada cuedta 10 soles. Dentro hay venta de artesanía, comida y be idas regionales.

text_clean: Lugar turístico para bañarse y relajarse. Usualmente es medio día de tour, complementado con canotaje en el río mayo por la tarde o castillo de lamas (les recomiendo el primero). En realidad, esta catarata es demasiado concurrida y no hay mucho espacio para colocar las cosas de uno. El agua es helada y refrescante y para poder tomarse fotos debajo de la caída hay que hacer cola debido a la concurrencia de gente. No la recomiendo más que para conocer rápidamente, preferí mucho más el tour a las cataratas de pishurayacu (caminata un poco agotadora de 45 minutos) o las cataratas de huacamaillo (caminata normal de 1 hora y media) por su poca concurrencia de gente.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: negativo

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: Stepping into the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary is like entering a giant stone Sudoku puzzle built by celestial civil engineers. Terraces rise like ancient ziggurats, water channels still flow with supernatural precision, and massive boulders cling to mountainsides as if Newton had never existed. You climb , slowly, dramatically, gasping , not just from altitude but awe. Every step upward is a stairway to cosmic confusion. How did they move these stones? Why do the sunbeams hit exactly the right notch on the solstice? And why is that random llama staring into my soul? The Temple of the Sun? Part sun dial, part cosmic WiFi router. The granaries perched across the valley? Possibly Incan refrigerators, possibly alien Airbnb. 👣 Pro tip: Start early. Tourists swarm like guinea pigs on coca leaf. But by 7:30am, it’s just you, the stones, and the whispers of empires past.

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
label: positivo

text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. text_clean: Situated on the outskirts of the historic city of Ollantaytambo, the Ollantaytambo Archaeological Site stands as one of the most captivating and beautiful destinations in the Sacred Valley. Offering panoramic views and captivating walks amidst ancient Inca architecture, the site boasts remnants of the legendary Templo del Sol (Temple of the Sun) and terraces dating back to the Inca era, originally built for agricultural purposes. During the Inca period, Ollantaytambo served as a crucial 'tambo,' a resting place for travelers along the Inca Trail. It marked the final stop before continuing the journey to Machu Picchu, as well as the first stop upon departing from the famed citadel. Additionally, it functioned as a vital storage facility for grains and crops. Steeped in history, Ollantaytambo also played a significant role during the Spanish conquest, serving as a stronghold for the Inca resistance. During our visit, we were mesmerized by the site's magic, spending approximately 45 to 60 minutes exploring its wonders. While souvenir and collectible shops line the entrance gate, unfortunately, we didn't have the opportunity to peruse them.
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: neutro

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: Stepping into the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary is like entering a giant stone Sudoku puzzle built by celestial civil engineers. Terraces rise like ancient ziggurats, water channels still flow with supernatural precision, and massive boulders cling to mountainsides as if Newton had never existed. You climb , slowly, dramatically, gasping , not just from altitude but awe. Every step upward is a stairway to cosmic confusion. How did they move these stones? Why do the sunbeams hit exactly the right notch on the solstice? And why is that random llama staring into my soul? The Temple of the Sun? Part sun dial, part cosmic WiFi router. The granaries perched across the valley? Possibly Incan refrigerators, possibly alien Airbnb. 👣 Pro tip: Start early. Tourists swarm like guinea pigs on coca leaf. But by 7:30am, it’s just you, the stones, and the whispers of empires past.

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).

text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).
label: neutro

text_clean: Lugar turístico para bañarse y relajarse. Usualmente es medio día de tour, complementado con canotaje en el río mayo por la tarde o castillo de lamas (les recomiendo el primero). En realidad, esta catarata es demasiado concurrida y no hay mucho espacio para colocar las cosas de uno. El agua es helada y refrescante y para poder tomarse fotos debajo de la caída hay que hacer cola debido a la concurrencia de gente. No la recomiendo más que para conocer rápidamente, preferí mucho más el tour a las cataratas de pishurayacu (caminata un poco agotadora de 45 minutos) o las cataratas de huacamaillo (caminata normal de 1 hora y media) por su poca concurrencia de gente.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: neutro

It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).
label: neutro

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: Situated on the outskirts of the historic city of Ollantaytambo, the Ollantaytambo Archaeological Site stands as one of the most captivating and beautiful destinations in the Sacred Valley. Offering panoramic views and captivating walks amidst ancient Inca architecture, the site boasts remnants of the legendary Templo del Sol (Temple of the Sun) and terraces dating back to the Inca era, originally built for agricultural purposes. During the Inca period, Ollantaytambo served as a crucial 'tambo,' a resting place for travelers along the Inca Trail. It marked the final stop before continuing the journey to Machu Picchu, as well as the first stop upon departing from the famed citadel. Additionally, it functioned as a vital storage facility for grains and crops. Steeped in history, Ollantaytambo also played a significant role during the Spanish conquest, serving as a stronghold for the Inca resistance. During our visit, we were mesmerized by the site's magic, spending approximately 45 to 60 minutes exploring its wonders. While souvenir and collectible shops line the entrance gate, unfortunately, we didn't have the opportunity to peruse them.
label: neutro

text_clean: Un lugar mágico. Accesible y barato. Si estás en Tarapoto, toma una moto al pequeño terminal de colectivos que se encuentra en el distrito Banda de Shilcayo (a 4 minutos de la Plaza de Armas de Tarspoto o a 5 minutos del aeropuerto). Luego, toma una de las camionetas que se van a barranquita y pagas 10 soles (cerca de tres dólares) para que te deje en las cataratas de Aguasiyacu. El viaje demora entre 20 y 30 minutos. La entrada cuedta 10 soles. Dentro hay venta de artesanía, comida y be idas regionales.

text_clean: Lugar turístico para bañarse y relajarse. Usualmente es medio día de tour, complementado con canotaje en el río mayo por la tarde o castillo de lamas (les recomiendo el primero). En realidad, esta catarata es demasiado concurrida y no hay mucho espacio para colocar las cosas de uno. El agua es helada y refrescante y para poder tomarse fotos debajo de la caída hay que hacer cola debido a la concurrencia de gente. No la recomiendo más que para conocer rápidamente, preferí mucho más el tour a las cataratas de pishurayacu (caminata un poco agotadora de 45 minutos) o las cataratas de huacamaillo (caminata normal de 1 hora y media) por su poca concurrencia de gente.

text_clean: Fantastic site, but really poor information for foreign tourists. You must enter the site as part of a tour and be ready to go at the prescribed time. This was communicated to me in rapid Spanish when purchasing a ticket, which I misunderstood as my slot time for entrance. The result was that I missed the start of the tour, had to chase the group through the site and totally miss the highlight (the exterior friezes) as the guards were hurrying me along, all so I could listen to a guide in a language I have fragmented understanding of. I certainly wouldn't expect people to speak English in this part of the world, but a simple sign in English at the ticket office explaining the requirement to be on a tour is a very easy solution!

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: positivo

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.
label: negativo
input_modelo: aspecto: accesibilidad reseña: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: It was beautiful. We were on a tour and learned a bit of the history of Peru and the highly advanced Inca Empire of South America in architecture, cultivation, and aqueducts to the Nasca lines. The people are shy, but friendly and courtesy once we got to know them on a personal level. Their civeche are hands down the best I have ever tried so far. We had a wonderful experience. And are planning to return to visit Machu Picchu and I, to seek spiritual awakening. Don't make the mistake that we did and make reservations for Machu Picchu months in advance because tickets sellout, for transportation, I recommend the luxury buses and be prepared for the two-miles-high club hike and altitude sickness. Have a great trip and a happy, prosperous life!

text_clean: Stepping into the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary is like entering a giant stone Sudoku puzzle built by celestial civil engineers. Terraces rise like ancient ziggurats, water channels still flow with supernatural precision, and massive boulders cling to mountainsides as if Newton had never existed. You climb , slowly, dramatically, gasping , not just from altitude but awe. Every step upward is a stairway to cosmic confusion. How did they move these stones? Why do the sunbeams hit exactly the right notch on the solstice? And why is that random llama staring into my soul? The Temple of the Sun? Part sun dial, part cosmic WiFi router. The granaries perched across the valley? Possibly Incan refrigerators, possibly alien Airbnb. 👣 Pro tip: Start early. Tourists swarm like guinea pigs on coca leaf. But by 7:30am, it’s just you, the stones, and the whispers of empires past.

text_clean: We had a terrible experience here. It is very hard to find any valuable descriptions online so we wanted to hire a guide. It was super hard to find anybody who spoke English, and the one who appeared finally asked for 120 SOLs, which is ridiculous. We wanted to join a group but it was not possible. The place in the afternoon is a no-go. It is overcrowded with tourists, you cannot enjoy anything. The sun shines from behind the hill, so for one, the site is already in shade, which is not pretty, and it shines in your face so you cannot see anything. Don’t be like us, go in the morning. Half of the site was closed which was not even indicated just up on the hill. Without a guide in bad lighting conditions they are just stones, it is not pretty, and the crowd is annoying. Worst historical site visit I ever had.

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
label: neutro

text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: Se puede llegar en teleferico o en carro demoran 20 min y 1:30 horas aprox respectivamen, hace bastante frío, se puede subir a caballo o a pie que es una caminata de 25 min aprox tiene lugarea donde descansar, en la parte donde se contrata el caballo se puede comer algo hay de diversos precios y gustos, al llegar a la fortaleza se puede contratar un guia que seria lo recomendable por lo general se forma un grupo grande para que el precio por persona sea mas accesible nos cobraron unos 60 soles el servicio de guia a todo el grupo, recomiendo ir a primer turno porque no hay muchas personas y se tiene más libertad de sacar fotos, cosa a resaltar se debe respetar el sendero y que te pueden sacar de la fortaleza por un incumplimiento de normas (le pasó a una pareja que venia detras nuestro). En general una vista increible llena de historia y cultura.
label: positivo
input_modelo: aspecto: atencion_servicio reseña: Se puede llegar en teleferico o en carro demoran 20 min y 1:30 horas aprox respectivamen, hace bastante frío, se puede subir a caballo o a pie que es una caminata de 25 min aprox tiene lugarea donde descansar, en la parte donde se contrata el caballo se puede comer algo hay de diversos precios y gustos, al llegar a la fortaleza se puede contratar un guia que seria lo recomendable por lo general se forma un grupo grande para que el precio por persona sea mas accesible nos cobraron unos 60 soles el servicio de guia a todo el grupo, recomiendo ir a primer turno porque no hay muchas personas y se tiene más libertad de sacar fotos, cosa a resaltar se debe respetar el sendero y que te pueden sacar de la fortaleza por un incumplimiento de normas (le pasó a una pareja que venia detras nuestro). En general una vista increible llena de historia y cultura.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: Lugar turístico para bañarse y relajarse. Usualmente es medio día de tour, complementado con canotaje en el río mayo por la tarde o castillo de lamas (les recomiendo el primero). En realidad, esta catarata es demasiado concurrida y no hay mucho espacio para colocar las cosas de uno. El agua es helada y refrescante y para poder tomarse fotos debajo de la caída hay que hacer cola debido a la concurrencia de gente. No la recomiendo más que para conocer rápidamente, preferí mucho más el tour a las cataratas de pishurayacu (caminata un poco agotadora de 45 minutos) o las cataratas de huacamaillo (caminata normal de 1 hora y media) por su poca concurrencia de gente.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: neutro

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: We had a terrible experience here. It is very hard to find any valuable descriptions online so we wanted to hire a guide. It was super hard to find anybody who spoke English, and the one who appeared finally asked for 120 SOLs, which is ridiculous. We wanted to join a group but it was not possible. The place in the afternoon is a no-go. It is overcrowded with tourists, you cannot enjoy anything. The sun shines from behind the hill, so for one, the site is already in shade, which is not pretty, and it shines in your face so you cannot see anything. Don’t be like us, go in the morning. Half of the site was closed which was not even indicated just up on the hill. Without a guide in bad lighting conditions they are just stones, it is not pretty, and the crowd is annoying. Worst historical site visit I ever had.

text_clean: When I first entered, I was underwhelmed. But after walking around for a while and seeing the different fountains, I realised that there was actually more there than I first anticipated. The main fountains varied their patterns over time, keeping interest alive. They varied from strong jets of water to spray effects and the lighting added further interest. The main Magic Fountain has incredibly powerful jets that shoot water up very high at times and the patterns vary over time well. There is also a fun tunnel made from water that people can walk through, but watch out for idiots that like to disrupt the water jets by putting their hands on the way in order to try to wet other visitors. But the main attraction is the 120 metre Fantasy Fountain, which has a display every 45 minutes or so. During this time, the fountains create a spray onto which projectors display an interesting film about the history of the country. This is mixed with music, lighting and other water jets to very good effect. It only cost 4 Soles to enter and they accept credit cards also. As you might expect, there are also food and drink vendors and toilets inside. There is also a fountain that people can go inside and play in (and get soaking wet).
label: neutro

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.
label: neutro

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo
